docs.title: about
docs.text: Topicalia is a lightweight web application that takes a slightly different approach to studying foreign languages, with Brazilian Portuguese as its showcase - partly by design, partly by passion. Below is a glimpse of what has taken shape so far.

docs.title: content
docs.text:
The material follows the rough outline of a course book series, though not too strictly:

  • A1-C2 levels loosely aligned with CEFR
  • expandable units per level
  • short, focused lessons, up to ten per unit
  • thematic indexes - referred to as coverages

Each lesson is accompanied by student reactions, some coming from fixed, named characters (personas), others appearing briefly as anonymous moods.

In this way, Topicalia is both a course and its commentary, while readers are watching a small class work through the material, occasionally more closely than the material itself would suggest.

the class is watching

docs.title: students
docs.text:
students roster - personas and moods

Personas are consistent enough to be recognizable, each carrying a particular bias or habit that tends to surface across lessons, sometimes drifting beyond the immediate topic. Moods, by contrast, appear briefly - short, anonymous reactions that register an impression and then disappear.

Inês in the sky with idioms

docs.title: coverages
docs.text:
Because the content is marked semantically, Topicalia can generate indexes of what actually appears across lessons - grammar areas, exercise types, lesson categories and selected highlights that would otherwise remain scattered.

coverage of Portuguese grammar

docs.title: highlights
docs.text:
Commonly marked words receive their own visual treatment:

highlights

Idioms, collocations and registers tend to stand out. One category is reserved for language-specific features; in Portuguese, this becomes the subjunctive, which quietly accumulates its own coverage as it appears.

highlighted subjunctive use for Portuguese material

docs.title: AI use
docs.text: Content is currently AI-generated and then curated by hand, though not in a way that hands authorship over entirely. The intention is to keep AI somewhere in the loop - useful, visible but not quite in charge.

docs.title: what this is not
docs.text:
Topicalia is not meant to become:

  • a gamified app (unless achievements remain tied to actual learning)
  • a grammar compendium
  • a chatbot tutor

docs.title: content development
docs.text:
There is room to grow in at least two directions, which may or may not converge.

  • top-downshaping levels into a clearer CEFR-aligned syllabus
  • grassrootsopening the repository to contributions - lessons, voices, even new languages

docs.title: application
docs.text:
As a single-person project, with tons of educational content to develop, Topicalia application is parked at MVP phase. A few paths remain open, should time allow:

  • FastAPIa natural step beyond Flask
  • databasepossibly MongoDB or a similar lightweight store
  • testingfirst manual, then web UI automation
  • hostingexploring minimal hosting setups for local preview and public delivery

docs.title: where to go next
docs.text:
If you want to understand the underlying logic - or at least the atmosphere around it - read:

  • why gonzo

For more about the author, visit:

  • LinkedIn
  • GitHub

docs.title: why gonzo?
docs.text:
The term inevitably points back to Hunter S. Thompson, whose name is inseparable from gonzo journalism - a form of reporting that places the reporter inside the story and, rather than pretending to stand at a neutral distance, moves through it with a mix of subjectivity, participation, satire and exaggeration that makes the act of observing indistinguishable from the act of altering.

That lineage matters, though only up to a point. Topicalia is not interested in imitating Thompson's prose, his politics or his persona so much as in borrowing something quieter and more structural - the sense that the observer alters what is being observed simply by being there and that voice is not an accessory to information but part of it.

Commentary is part of the event.

There is also a second, less deliberate lineage behind the name: Gonzo from The Muppet Show, a character who never quite settles into the category assigned to him and who performs acts that are unnecessary, excessive, occasionally absurd and carried out with little concern for whether they will be understood at all. He does not clarify situations so much as intensifies them, pushing things slightly off balance, making them stranger and therefore easier to notice. That impulse - toward the unnecessary but revealing gesture - has its place here as well, where not every voice is required to explain and some are allowed to distort, exaggerate or simply remain at an angle to the lesson, which is often where meaning begins to show itself.

docs.title: the second inheritance
docs.text:
Anthony Bourdain belongs here for a different reason. He was not a gonzo journalist in any strict sense but he practiced a related kind of presence, moving through food, cities, eateries and local customs without much interest in appearing objective.

What matters here is not cuisine or celebrity but method: the way he entered spaces, listened, reacted, digressed, judged, admired and occasionally recoiled, without ever suggesting that the world was waiting to be arranged into neat pedagogical categories. That rhythm - not polished explanation but situated presence - is close to what Topicalia draws from its auxiliary voices.

docs.title: what gonzo means inside Topicalia
docs.text:
A lesson may say one thing plainly, a persona may restate it more diplomatically and a mood may push it toward something harsher, stranger, or unintentionally revealing; the core content remains stable but the field around it shifts just enough to make the same material behave differently under pressure.

This is why the word "gonzo" still earns its place, suggesting that the lesson is never quite a sealed object but something unfolding in the presence of voices that observe, interfere and occasionally distort it as it takes shape.

docs.title: further reading
docs.text:
If you want to follow the thread a bit further:

  • Uncovering the Origins of "Gonzo" on the 20th Anniversary of Hunter S. Thompson’s Death
  • Anthony Bourdain - Parts Unknown
  • Anthony Bourdain - selected interviews
  • Gonzo - The Muppet Show clips
